range.nativeRange: Europe, Asia, and North America — native across much of the Northern Hemisphere; native throughout eastern North America and Pennsylvania

usNativeStatus.description: Native across most of the lower 48 states, though the picture is complex — populations in western North America (California, Pacific Northwest, Rocky Mountains) are generally considered native; eastern US populations are a mix of native and Eurasian-naturalized strains that have introgressed over centuries; genetically difficult to distinguish in the wild; treated as native throughout the US for most conservation and garden purposes

invasive.description: Not invasive in the US — yarrow is broadly native or naturalized throughout North America; it spreads by rhizomes and self-seeds but is not considered invasive; its spread is manageable and ecologically appropriate in most settings

description.spread: 18-24"; spreads by rhizomes to form colonies over time
description.growthRate: Fast; establishes readily; spreads moderately by rhizome
description.timeToMaturity: Full bloom from first year; spreads to full colony size in years 2-3
description.lifespan: Long-lived; essentially permanent as a colony
description.deciduousEvergreen: Semi-evergreen — ferny foliage often persists through mild winters; one of the early emergers in spring
description.winterInterest: Low to moderate — dried flat-topped seedheads persist through winter; the feathery foliage sometimes visible; modest winter interest

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun preferred; tolerates part shade with reduced bloom; full sun produces the most fragrant and medicinal plants
lightAndWater.water: Low; excellent drought tolerance — one of the most drought-tolerant perennials available; thrives in hot, dry conditions

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained to dry; performs in lean, dry soils; does not tolerate waterlogged conditions; excessive moisture promotes floppy growth and root rot

claySoilPerformance.description: Tolerant but clay creates risk — heavy clay that retains moisture through wet periods can cause root rot and stem flop; yarrow performs better in clay that has been amended with coarse grit, gravel, or compost to open drainage; raised planting positions (berms, mounds, raised beds) help significantly; on-grade clay planting works if the surface drains within 24-48 hours of rain; do not mulch heavily around the crown

soil.texturePreference: Lean to average; does not require fertile soil; thrives in poor conditions; excess fertility produces floppy growth
soil.rootCharacter: Creeping rhizomatous root system; spreads steadily; also a dynamic mineral accumulator via deep fibrous roots that mine trace minerals

bloom.bloomTime: June through September — one of the longer-blooming perennials; often reblooms if deadheaded
bloom.bloomDescription: Flat-topped flower clusters (corymbs) 3-5" wide; tiny individual flowers (white in species, also pink, red, yellow in cultivars); the flat top is highly accessible to beneficial insects; at peak bloom the plant is covered in white flower platforms
bloom.fragrance: Strongly aromatic foliage — sharp, medicinal, camphor-like; distinctive; the aroma is stronger from the foliage than the flowers
bloom.pollination: One of the most ecologically important insectary plants — the accessible flat flower structure is usable by the widest possible range of insects; parasitic wasps (Braconid, Ichneumonid, Chalcid), hoverflies, soldier beetles, ground beetles, lacewings, native bees, honeybees, butterflies, and many other beneficial and neutral insects all use yarrow intensively; arguably the single most important insectary plant for a temperate food garden

yield.edibleParts: Leaves (mildly bitter herb; used in herbal teas; young leaves in salads — use sparingly due to bitterness); flowers (mild; used as garnish)
yield.harvestSeason: Leaves spring through summer; flowers at peak bloom; dry immediately after harvesting
yield.medicinalUses: Outstanding traditional wound-healing herb — the genus name references the mythological hero Achilles who used it to staunch wounds; documented as an anti-inflammatory, styptic (stops bleeding), mild antiseptic, and antimicrobial; the plant contains salicylates, tannins, alkaloids, and essential oils; used for: wound healing (poultice), fever reduction (diaphoretic — promotes sweating), menstrual regulation, digestive support; one of the most widely used medicinal plants in European and North American traditional medicine; one of the priority plants for a home medicinal herb collection
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried flowers for arrangements (flat heads dry beautifully); yellow and gold dye from flowers; aromatic dried herb

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Generally non-toxic at culinary and medicinal doses; CAUTION: individuals allergic to ragweed (Asteraceae family) may have cross-reactivity; yarrow contains thujone — large quantities should be avoided during pregnancy (traditional emmenagogue); skin contact with the sap can cause photosensitivity (phototoxic reaction in sensitive individuals) — wash hands after handling
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Mildly toxic to dogs and cats — can cause GI upset, drooling, and skin irritation; toxic to horses if consumed in large quantities (not a realistic concern in normal garden settings)
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: The aromatic compounds have mild allelopathic effects on some neighboring plants; yarrow also produces root exudates that stimulate the growth of neighboring plants — traditionally described as a "soil activator" that improves the health of nearby plants (the permaculture concept of dynamic accumulator)

roles: Dynamic Mineral Accumulator (one of the best — accumulates potassium, calcium, silica, magnesium, and copper from deep in the soil; leaves returned to the soil cycle these minerals for neighboring plants)
Insectary Plant (primary — one of the most important beneficial insect plants in the garden; increases natural pest control throughout the food garden)
Medicinal (one of the priority medicinal herbs)
companionPlants: most vegetables and herbs; the insectary value makes yarrow beneficial to every plant in the garden; a single plant near a vegetable bed increases beneficial insect populations for the whole area; pairs well with echinacea
antagonists: None documented; yarrow is generally beneficial to neighboring plants
permaculture.coppiceable: Cut back after the first flush of bloom (July) to encourage a second flush; cut to the ground in late fall; divide spreading colonies every 3-4 years to keep productive

wildlife.attracts: Extraordinary range of beneficial insects — see Pollination notes; the single most insect-visited perennial in many garden surveys; essential infrastructure plant for natural pest management
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Painted lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui), American lady (V. virginiensis), and many other Lepidoptera use Achillea species as larval hosts
wildlife.nestingHabitat: Hollow dried stems provide overwintering habitat for small solitary bees; the leaf litter provides cover for ground beetles

deer.description: Excellent — aromatic foliage strongly deters deer; rarely browsed; one of the most reliable deer-deterrent plants for the garden

salt.description: Good — tolerates road salt spray; one of the plants that naturalizes along roadsides where salt spray is present

drought.description: Outstanding — among the most drought-tolerant perennials available; native to dry meadows and disturbed areas; thrives in conditions that stress most other plants

tolerances.other: Tolerates lean soil, neglect, and difficult growing conditions; essentially an indestructible garden plant when sited correctly

maintenance.pruningNotes: Deadhead spent flower clusters to promote rebloom. Cut back hard after the main summer bloom. Cut to the ground in fall or early spring. Divide spreading clumps every 3-4 years when the center becomes sparse. Manage rhizome spread by pulling unwanted runners.
propagationMethods: Division (easy — divide in spring or fall; the most reliable method)
seed (easy; direct sow in spring or fall; self-sows moderately)

cultivarNotes: The straight white-flowered species is the most important medicinal and insectary form; named cultivars trade some of these properties for color variation. **'Moonshine'** (A. 'Moonshine' — light yellow, compact 18", excellent insectary value, zones 3-8); **'Coronation Gold'** (A. filipendulina — taller golden yellow, striking in arrangements, zones 3-9); **'Paprika'** (red-orange, fades to pink, zones 3-9); **'Cerise Queen'** (cherry red, zones 3-9). For permaculture use, the straight species or 'Moonshine' are the first choices; colored cultivars are fine in ornamental borders but the species retains the highest medicinal and insectary value.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Universal insectary anchor — yarrow is a primary constituent of every fruit tree guild and food forest understory; plant throughout the drip line of fruit and nut trees to draw parasitic wasps, hoverflies, and beneficial beetles that control pest populations across the entire garden; effective as a front-to-mid border plant in perennial beds; integrates into meadow plantings, herb gardens, and companion planting schemes; the flat flower structure serves the widest possible range of beneficial insects, making it valuable anywhere in the garden regardless of design style

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Yes — Achillea millefolium is native to Pennsylvania and the broader Appalachian region; commonly found in meadows, roadsides, and disturbed areas
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus: Not invasive in Pennsylvania — PA DCNR does not list Achillea millefolium as invasive; it is a native wildflower throughout the state
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay is workable for yarrow provided surface drainage is adequate — the sloping or graded portions of the site drain reasonably well; in-ground planting is generally fine; avoid low spots or areas where water pools after rain; no amendment strictly required but a handful of coarse grit worked in at planting improves establishment
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins lean red clay is actually well-suited to yarrow — the low fertility of construction-disturbed clay soil suits a plant that performs best in impoverished conditions; do not fertilize; the native soil is fine as planted
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: The straight species or 'Moonshine' are the best choices for Hegins — both are fully hardy, proven in Zone 6a, and offer the highest insectary and medicinal value; 'Moonshine' adds soft yellow color if desired for ornamental borders; the species is the default for guild plantings
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Apple guild — plant throughout the drip line of both apple trees as a primary insectary understory; Mulberry guild — include under the Illinois Everbearing mulberry for the same insectary function; Paw Paw guild — include at the edges of the paw paw pair where light permits (paw paw canopy is dense; site yarrow at the drip line margin); South-facing beds (Section C) — suitable as a drought-tolerant front-border perennial given the warm, dry microclimate; the species' native status makes it an appropriate choice for any location on the site
